segments.text: I'm about to show you the 10 most expensive mistakes intraday traders make.
And if you've ever chased a gap at the open, you've made at least one of these.
Every example in this video is a real trade from real traders in names like AMD,
NVIDIA, SNDK, and a bunch of other names.
Some of these mistakes look smart in the moment.
And then quietly drain your account for months.
But here's the twist.
The traders who fixed these mistakes didn't trade more.
They waited for better information.
By the end of this video, you'll never look at gaps the same way again.
So let's start with the trader who needed to be first and paid for it almost every single morning.
Number 10.
The immediate gap and fail.
This is a chaser trap.
The trader who needed to be first.
There was this trader on our desk who was always always.
He wanted to be early.
Not reckless.
He just wanted to be early.
He hated that feeling of watching a stock run without him, especially if it was an idea
that he had.
So when it stock gapped up and then ripped right off the open, he felt like the clock
was ticking.
He could feel that urge to get involved.
He had to, right?
It was his idea.
It was already going.
Every second that he waited felt like money slipping away right out of his hands.
He'd hit the buy button right near the highs and tell himself he'd keep the risk really
tight.
He had to win himself that what he was seeing in that up move was real momentum.
Almost inevitably as soon as he would get in the stock, it would stall.
And then it would start to fade.
He'd stop out.
He'd re-enter.
He'd stop out again.
What finally broke this big nasty pattern he was in.
It wasn't a big loss.
It was actually embarrassment.
He realized he was reacting to movement, not strength.
So now when he sees a stock gapped up and sprint immediately, he slows himself down.
He waits.
He waits to see if price can rest without it falling apart.
So every time it used to feel like urgency, now it just feels like a warning.
Now he feels the urgency and it triggers a totally different set of emotions.
Not one of action, but one of awareness.
He's like a sniper.
The target insight does not mean to take the shot right away.
It just means he has the target.
Now it's time to look for the shot to be sure it's there before he takes it.
So he focuses on the first pull in to see what happens there.
He waits for the information.
His eyes on the target.
When he feels that urgency, it's some little piece of information telling him to be alert,
but he waits for the information to give him the opportunity.
And sometimes the opportunity comes in a different way than what he expected.
So in an example like we're looking at, when it gets sold and then sold again, that urgency
that used to just stop him out, frustrate him, really get him on his heels for the rest
of the day.
That's where he used to completely get run over.
But now he can reset.
He can wait and he can find when it gets and holds below a key support area if this stock
is going to reverse.
That's a whole different trade with a whole different opportunity.
When the bank gets his direct deposit from his trading account at the end of the month,
they don't ask, did you make this money long chasing momentum?
Or did you wait for that momentum to curl and then short it?
That's not the way it works.
Traders make more by taking the clear trades for that.
Number nine, the gap up into higher time frame resistance.
This is one that drives traders crazy.
It's the pain of not zooming out.
And one trader on our desk, he wants believed that the open told the whole story.
So one morning a stock gaped up 5%.

He bought the first pullback, but then he got instantly rejected.
It was frustrating, right?
Only after did he notice something almost embarrassing.
The stock had opened directly into a daily resistance level that had rejected at a prior
price for almost weeks.
That loss, that only loss right there.
He didn't have to make a bunch of mistakes, but that one allowed him to rewire his process.
Now every time he's looking at a stock that gaps up, it starts with one question.
What problem does this gap run into?
What we're talking about is a stock that opens up right into a really important resistance
level.
When he sees that gap pushing into a higher time frame supply area, an area that's been
rejected previously, ideally multiple times or gapping right into an area that was rejected
significantly earlier if you look back on the chart.
He no longer expects continuation.
He no longer expects that gap to be the catalyst that drives it forward.
He expects, rightfully so, negotiation.
And he's open to the idea that this could fail.

overleaning in either direction.

that every single gap up into resistance is going to run from that level.
He's giving himself the opportunity to take the trade that's being offered.
Not the trade that he initially expected.
This trader prepares for both directions every time he's looking for a gap into resistance
regardless of how good or bad the pre-market price action looks before the open.

with no follow through.

doesn't trade enough volume or gaps up.

b, is there clearly defined trend, either uptrend or downtrend?
